coredata.dc:title: Biomass carbon dots-assisted Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composites with Co-P electron-bridge for enhanced photocatalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tao
Wang, Jinyu
Li, Chunlan
Wu, Yu
Sun, Yuxuan
Liu, Xiqing
Su, Xiaoli
Zhan, Hongquan
Wang, Yongqing
coredata.dc:description: Carbon materials can act as electron transfer media to effectively enhance photocatalytic performance. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using green tea to modify Ni2P/NiCo-LDH heterostructures. Benefitting from the Co-P bond formed between Ni2P and NiCo-LDH and biomass CDs, charge transfer was improved via dual channel. Ni2P and CDs acted as active sites for improving the photocatalytic performance. Owing to the synergistic process, the photoresponse capability of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH was significantly increased. In addition, the possible active species in the photocatalytic process are discussed. This study provides a design for ternary photocatalysts with carbon materials and phosphides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass carbon
Electron-bridge
CDs
NiCo-LDH
Ni2P

GJJ211339 , GJJ211340 , GJJ150905 ) and the Jingdezhen Science and Technology Bureau Project (Grant Nos. 20224SF005–06 , 20224SF005–09 , 20202GYZD013 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of as-prepared samples (a) and EDS spectra of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH (b).
TEM images of NiCo-LDH (a), Ni2P (b) and CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH (c), HRTEM image of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH (d).
FT-IR spectra (a), XPS survey spectra (b), Co 2p (c), Ni 2p (d), C 1 s (e) and P 2p (f) high-resolution XPS spectra.
UV–vis DRS (a) and N2 adsorption-desorption plots (b).
I-t curves (a) and EIS spectra (b) of NiCo-LDH, Ni2P/NiCo-LDH and CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH.

Photocatalytic performance of as-obtained samples (a) and corresponding pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics (b). the cycling experiments (c), XRD pattern before and after photocatalytic experiments (d) over 5% CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composite.
Trapping experiments with the addition of different sacrificial agents over 5% CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composite.
The possible photocatalytic process over CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composite.

head.ce:title: Biomass carbon dots-assisted Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composites with Co-P electron-bridge for enhanced photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jingdezhen Ceramic University, Jingdezhen 333403, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Materials Science and Engineering, Jingdezhen Ceramic University, Jingdezhen 333403, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon materials can act as electron transfer media to effectively enhance photocatalytic performance. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using green tea to modify Ni2P/NiCo-LDH heterostructures. Benefitting from the Co-P bond formed between Ni2P and NiCo-LDH and biomass CDs, charge transfer was improved via dual channel. Ni2P and CDs acted as active sites for improving the photocatalytic performance. Owing to the synergistic process, the photoresponse capability of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH was significantly increased. In addition, the possible active species in the photocatalytic process are discussed. This study provides a design for ternary photocatalysts with carbon materials and phosphides.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing amount of organic pollutants in water sources, the development of water treatment methods for removing contaminants has attracted considerable attention [1–4]. Photocatalysis, an advanced oxidation process, is an environmentally friendly, simple, and sustainable treatment method. Compared with biodegradation and chemical treatments, photocatalysis can be used for complex and highly concentrated organic contaminations without the addition of oxidizing gents [5–8]. During photocatalysis, photocatalysts absorb the photon energy from sunlight to generate electron–hole (e −-h +) pairs. The pollutants are degraded by reactive oxygen species, such as O2 −,‧OH, and 1O2, formed by e −-h + pairs. TiO2 and ZnO are considered the most attractive photocatalysts [9,10]. However, the low light absorption and wide bandgap limit the activities of TiO2 and ZnO.

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs), N-type materials [11], also called hydrotalcites, are non-conventional semiconductors with layered structures and formula [M2+1-x M3+ x/n(OH)2] [An− x/n.H2O] [12]. LDHs exhibit outstanding performance in the photocatalytic field owing to their large specific surface area, adjustable band structure, narrow band gap, and abundant active sites [12–14]. To further develop LDHs for photocatalytic applications, various strategies to improve their catalytic ability have been developed. Zhang et al. [15] constructed a BiVO4@LDH heterojunction for photocatalytic degradation. The results of density functional theory demonstrated that a built-in electric field was formed, which accelerated the migration of electrons. Tao et al. [16] obtained a MoS2/CoAl-LDH composite via a hydrothermal process; the composite exhibited an enhanced separation of interfacial charge carriers for photocatalytic reactivity via an internal electric field. Therefore, a suitable method can be used to improve the photocatalytic activity of LDH.

Suitable co-catalysts are of great use to accelerate the migration of photo-induced charge carriers. Precious metals (Pt, Au) are usually applied to enhance photocatalytic activity [17–19]. However, their scarce reserves and high prices limit their use as cocatalysts. Recently, Ni-based co-catalysts, such as nickel sulfide, hydroxide phosphide, and metallic nickel, have been used to improve photocatalytic activity [20–22]. Among these, nickel phosphides, which are N-type materials [23], have been developed as next-generation co-catalysts instead of Pt, owing to their abundance, optoelectronic properties, and unique crystalline structures. In our previous work [24], a Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composite was synthesized; the composite exhibited high photocatalytic activity because of the Co-P bond acting as a unique charge transfer channel. However, the optimum Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composite still exhibited a low degradation rate.

Carbon dots (CDs) can speed up the charge migration in photocatalysts owing to their high electrical conductivity [25–28]. Moreover, CDs, as novel carbon materials, exhibit several outstanding properties, including non-toxicity, photo-induced charge transfer, and ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption. For example, Hu et al. [29] demonstrated that the introduction of CDs in BiCOOH/g-C3N4 enhanced the photocatalytic activity. Jiang et al. [30] modified the ZnIn2S4/BiOCl heterojunction with CDs, which accelerated the charge transfer and improved the photocatalytic activity under full-spectrum light irradiation. Therefore, we hypothesize that the use of CDs as electron receivers and reactive sites can further enhance the charge transfer and photocatalytic activity.

Herein, CDs were synthesized using green tea and were then used to modify the Ni2P/NiCo-LDH heterostructures. The resultant CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH exhibited evidently enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance compared with that of Ni2P/NiCo-LDH under the same conditions. The improvement in photocatalytic activity was because of the Co-P bond and CDs promoting charge separation and transfer, as well as acting as reactive sites to accelerate the photocatalytic reaction. In addition, the photoresponse of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH was significantly enhanced by CD modification. Finally, a possible photocatalytic mechanism is discussed in detail. This work provides a new direction for designing excellent photocatalysts.

Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), citric acid (CA), ascorbic acid (ASC), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate, and sodium hypophosphite monohydrate were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Inc. (Shanghai, China). Nickel (II) hydroxide, nickel nitrate hexahydrate, ammonium chloride, sodium hydroxide, ethanol (EtOH), and tetracycline (TC) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Green tea was purchased from a supermarket. Deionized water was prepared in the laboratory and used in all experiments. All reagents were used without further purification.

Ni2P was synthesized according to the method described in a previous study [31]. Ni(OH)2 (1 mmol) and NaH2PO2 (0.75 mmol) were mixed and ground well. The mixture was then calcined at 300 °C for 300 min with N2 protection. The samples were washed with deionized water and ethanol and then dried for further experiments.
The CDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method using green tea [32]. The green tea was soaked in an EtOH solution. After treatment with ultrasound, the solids were removed to obtain the CD solution.

The Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composite having optimum activity was prepared according to our previous work [24]. Ni(NO3)3‧6H2O, Co(NO3)3‧6H2O, and NH4Cl were dissolved in 20 mL with strong stirring. Subsequently, a NaOH solution (0.2 M) was slowly added to the mixture and the mixture was then heated to 55 °C. After the reaction proceeded for 3 h, Ni2P was added and the reaction continued for 15 h. Finally, the powder was washed with water and EtOH, and then dried for subsequent experiments. Pure NiCo-LDH was thus obtained without the addition of Ni2P.

CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH was synthesized via adsorption utilizing the surface groups of materials. Ni2P/NiCo-LDH (0.5 g) was added into a centrifuge tube with the CD solution, and EtOH was poured to a total volume of 10 mL. After ultrasonication for 30 min, the samples were washed with EtOH and dried. By changing the volume of the CDs added, a series of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composites was obtained, such that the theoretical mass ratio of CDs was 3, 5, 7, and 10 wt%; the samples were denoted as 3% CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH, 5% CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH, 7% CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH, and 10% CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH, respectively.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) experiments were conducted to determine the crystal structures of the composites. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded to observe the morphologies. Fourier transform spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to determine the chemical groups present in the samples. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were plotted to calculate the specific surface area. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to analyze the chemical state of the elements. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) was applied to analyze the light absorption. Electrochemical properties were investigated using an electrochemical analyzer (CHI760e).

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples was evaluated by TC degradation under visible-light irradiation. The sample (10 mg) was first dispersed into 100 mL of TC (10 mg L−1) solution in the dark for 60 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. After the light was turned on, 4 mL of the suspension was collected after a certain duration and the sample was removed through a 0.045 μm membrane. The concentration of TC in the liquid was determined using a UV–vis spectrometer.

The XRD patterns of the as-prepared samples are shown in Fig. 1 a. The diffraction peaks of NiCo-LDH at 11.5°, 23.1°, 33.4°, 38,6°, and 59.5° are ascribed to the lattice planes of (003), (006), (009), (012), and (110), respectively. For Ni2P/NiCo-LDH, four new diffraction peaks appear at 40.8°, 44.6°, 47.5°, and 54.3°, which are attributed to the (111), (201), (210), and (300) lattice planes of Ni2P, respectively, indicating the successful preparation of Ni2P/NiCo-LDH. After the CDs are used for further modifications, no new peaks appear in the XRD patterns of the CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH, implying the low content of CDs with uniform dispersion. The EDS spectrum of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH (Fig. 1b) suggests that presence of C, Co, Ni, and P in the composite. Moreover, additional characterizations were used to investigate the properties of the CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH.

The morphologies of the as-obtained samples were observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, NiCo-LDH exhibits a thin 3D flower-like structure, which provides more load sites. The particles of Ni2P are approximately 20 nm in size and are agglomerated together. After the addition of Ni2P and CDs to NiCo-LDH, the Ni2P nanoparticles deposit on the surface of NiCo-LDH without CDs because CDs may have entered the interior of the sample owing to their small size. In addition, the HRTEM images of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH (Fig. 2b) show a lattice spacing of 0.38 nm, corresponding to the (311) phase of Ni2P, suggesting the successful preparation of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH, which is consistent with the XRD results.

The chemical groups present in the samples were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy [33]. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the absorption bands at 3437 and 1621 cm−1 in all samples correspond to the stretching and bending vibrations of the OH bond. For NiCo-LDH, the chemical band at 1392 cm−1 is assigned to the symmetric stretching vibration of the inserted anion. For CDs, the peak at 1392 cm−1 is ascribed to the CH bending vibration. For Ni2P, the unique chemical band at 1051 cm−1 is assigned to the P-O group [34]. The absorption peaks at 500–1100 cm−1 are attributed to the lattice vibration of the metal-oxygen bond (Ni/Co-O) for Ni2P and NiCo-LDH. The peak of NiCo-LDH at 649 cm−1 is assigned to the metal-oxygen-metal bonds (Co-O-Co, Ni-O-Ni, and Co-O-Ni). For CDs, the absorption peaks at 500–1100 cm−1 are mainly attributed to CO and C=O bonds. After the modification of NiCo-LDH by CDs and Ni2P, all the abovementioned peaks are observed in the FT-IR spectra of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH. However, the shift in the peak positions suggests an interaction between the chemical bonds of the materials.

The XPS technique was used to investigate the elemental oxidation states. Fig. 3b–f show the XPS survey and typical Ni, Co, P, and C spectra fitted via a Gaussian function. Shake-up satellites with mixed valences of Co2+/Co3+ and Ni2+/Ni3+ were characterized [35,36]. The XPS survey spectra shown in Fig. 3b indicate the presence of Co and Ni in NiCo-LDH. For CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH, a new P 2p peak is observed and the C 1 s peak is enhanced, suggesting the successful preparation of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH. The high-resolution XPS of Co 2p is shown in Fig. 3c. The Co 2p3/2 peak for NiCo-LDH is deconvoluted into 781.6 and 786.5 eV, suggesting the presence of Co2+ and Co3+ oxidation states. The peaks of Co 2p1/2, located at 796.9 and 803.3 eV, are attributed to Co3+ and Co2+, respectively. In addition, the peak at 775.2 eV for NiCo-LDH, is assigned to Coδ+. For CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH, the peaks are shifted to a higher energy level, indicating the formation of a strong interaction. As expected, a new Coδ+ peak appears, suggesting that NiCo-LDH is positively charged. The peaks of Ni 2p3/2 at 855.7 and 861.8 eV in NiCo-LDH are ascribed to Ni3+ and Ni2+, respectively (Fig. 3d). The binding energy peak of Ni 2p1/2 is deconvoluted into two peaks at 873.5 and 879.7 eV, suggesting the presence of Ni3+ and Ni2+ oxidation states. The peaks of Ni 2p in CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH are shifted to higher energy levels, further suggesting that the formation of a strong interaction. The peaks corresponding to the binding energy of C 1 s, located at 284.7 and 285.1 eV, are assigned to CC and CO/C=O, respectively (Fig. 3e). For the P 2p XPS spectra, the binding energy peaks at 133.0 and 133.8 eV are ascribed to P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 3f). The peak at 134.6 eV correspond to the oxides of P (POx) because of the presence of the oxygen functional groups of the synthetizations. Moreover, a weak binging energy peak at 131.1 eV is observed, which is ascribed to the negative valence states of P (Pδ−). According to previous reports [24,37], a Co-P bond forms between Coδ+ and Pδ−. The abovementioned results demonstrate that an interaction is formed in CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH, which promotes charge separation.

The light absorption abilities of the photocatalyst were investigated by UV–vis DRS. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the absorbance edges shift to longer wavelengths after the modification of NiCo-LDH with CDs and Ni2P, suggesting that more light is absorbed by CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH. Meanwhile, N2 adsorption–desorption curves were plotted to analyze the specific surface area of the photocatalyst. Generally, a larger surface area implies more active sites for a photocatalytic process. As shown in Fig. 4b, the plots are type-III isotherms with H3 loops. The calculation results show that the specific surface areas of the Ni2P/NiCo-LDH and CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composites are 316.572 and 343.551 m2/g, respectively; the increase in the surface area is not considerable compared with that of NiCo-LDH. Therefore, the photocatalytic performance improvement of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composites is attributed to the light absorption and charge separation efficiencies.

The separation of photo-generated e −-h + pairs of the photocatalyst were determined by employing EIS (Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy) and measuring the photocurrent. From the I-t curves (Fig. 5 a), the photocurrent density of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH is considerably higher than those of Ni2P/NiCo-LDH and NiCo-LDH, indicating that CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH has a higher separation efficiency for photo-induced e −-h + pairs. In addition, EIS Nyquist curves were plotted to investigate the charge separation efficiency. As shown in Fig. 5b, the arc diameter of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH is considerably smaller than those of Ni2P/NiCo-LDH and Ni2P/NiCo-LDH, further confirming that CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH has the optimum separation efficiency for e −-h + pairs, implying that CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH exhibits enhanced photocatalytic performance.

Before the photocatalytic experiments, the adsorption experiments for all samples were performed under dark conditions. When the samples achieved adsorption-desorption equilibrium, the photocatalytic degradation of TC over all samples was evaluated under visible-light illumination. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the modification of CDs has a significant effect on the photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared samples. With increasing CD content, the photocatalytic activity is enhanced. The optimum reaction rate constant k (0.01514 cm−1) is achieved with the 5% CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composite, which is approximately 1.99 times than that of NiCo-LDH (0.00761 cm−1) and 1.41 times than that of Ni2P/NiCo-LDH (0.01073 cm−1). The enhanced photocatalytic activity is due to charge transfer caused by the introduction of CDs, Ni2P, and NiCo-LDH. The cycling tests show that the degradation rate is stable over the 5% CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composite after three cycles (Fig. 6c). The XRD pattern of the 5% CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composite is recorded (Fig. 6d). There are no evident changes in the XRD pattern, further suggesting that the photocatalyst has good stability. Therefore, the photocatalyst has the potential for practical applications.

To further investigate the photocatalytic process, trapping experiments were performed over an optimal sample with various sacrificial agents. As shown in Fig. 7 , the photocatalytic activity decreases with the additions of IPA (capture h +), AC (capture ‧OH), and ASC (capture O2 −), suggesting that all three active species play important roles during the photocatalytic process. It is known that the electric potentials of O2 .−/O2 and ‧OH/-OH are −0.33 and 1.99 eV, respectively. Additionally, the CB of NiCo-LDH is more negative than that of O2 .−/O2, whereas the VB of NiCo-LDH is lower than that of ‧OH/-OH. Therefore, O2 .− and h + act as the main active species in the photocatalytic process. However, trapping experiments demonstrate that ‧OH also participates in the photocatalytic reaction. We respected that the ‧OH was generated by O2 − reacted with -OH.

Based on the above analysis, a possible photocatalytic process was proposed (Fig. 8 ). Under light illumination, N-type NiCo-LDH is excited to generate e −-h + pairs. Because the VB of NiCo-LDH is lower than that of ‧OH/OH−, h + is directly involved in the oxidation of TC. Benefitting from the conductivity of CDs and Ni2P, the photo-induced e- quickly migrate to CDs and Ni2P to achieve fast and efficient separation of e −-h + pairs. Subsequently, e − reacts with O2 to generate O2 .−, which acts as an active species to oxidize TC. Simultaneously, O2 .− also reacts with OH− to generate ‧OH. Finally, TC is degraded by h +, O2 .−, and ‧OH.

This study investigated the preparation and photocatalytic performance of the CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composite for TC removal. A strong interaction formed in the CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composite, thereby promoting the charge transfer and separation capability. Owing to the synergy of the CDs and Ni2P, electrons could further separate and act as active sites. Therefore, the CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH composite exhibited increased photocatalytic activity. In addition, the photocatalytic mechanism of CDs/Ni2P/NiCo-LDH was proposed via trapping experiments for a detailed investigation of the photocatalytic application of NiCo-LDH materials.

Tao Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jinyu Wang: Data curation. Chunlan Li: Data curation. Yu Wu: Writing – review & editing. Yuxuan Sun: Writing – review & editing. Xiqing Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiaoli Su: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Hongquan Zhan: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yongqing Wang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52062020), Education Department of Jiangxi Province (Grant Nos. GJJ211339, GJJ211340, GJJ150905) and the Jingdezhen Science and Technology Bureau Project (Grant Nos. 20224SF005–06, 20224SF005–09, 20202GYZD013).
